title: Navigating Global Natural Gas Challenges
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 21 March 2026
content:
The world is currently facing its second major gas shock in half a decade due to widespread supply disruptions. After Russia cut flows to Europe in 2022 to undermine support for Ukraine, the continent turned to liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipped from America and the Middle East, causing prices to soar. Now, LNG supplies from the Gulf have also been interrupted, which has led to another surge in global power prices. In response, some experts argue that renewable energy sources offer a promising way to reduce reliance on gas and ensure greater energy security. They point to countries like Spain, which has heavily invested in wind and solar power, successfully limiting gas-driven power pricing to only 15% of the time this year.

Despite these advances, the world will likely remain haunted by the specter of natural gas shocks for decades to come. Even in a future dominated by clean energy, natural gas will continue to play a critical role in electricity generation. This is primarily because solar and wind power, along with current battery technologies, cannot offer complete energy security on their own. Analysts contend that it is possible to run a cost-effective electricity grid where the vast majority of power comes from renewables in concert with batteries. However, a grid split between 90% clean energy and 10% fossil fuels means that for a tenth of the time, nearly all power must be derived from fossil fuels.

The necessity of fossil fuel backups stems from the fact that renewables are ultimately at the mercy of the weather. While modern batteries can smooth out short-term fluctuations in energy supply and demand, they cannot keep the lights on for extended periods. Other options for continuous power are often unappealing or geographically constrained. Hydropower depends heavily on favorable weather, as demonstrated by Europe's 2022 drought, while new nuclear power stations remain prohibitively expensive. Furthermore, alternative forms of long-duration storage, such as iron-air batteries or hydrogen, are still in their infancy.

For the foreseeable future, analysts' net-zero projections must include a role for natural gas, leaving importers vulnerable to supply disruptions. To mitigate these risks, governments must maintain sufficient capacity to satisfy peak demand through import facilities, pipelines, and strategic reserves. Though gas cannot be entirely avoided, grids can be made larger and smarter to significantly reduce reliance on it. Implementing time-varying prices encourages consumers to shift their energy usage to hours when power comes from abundant, nearly free sources like the sun and wind. If prices rise when clean energy is scarce, it will naturally incentivize further investment in promising forms of energy storage.

Additionally, localized pricing can ensure that energy-intensive industries and storage facilities are situated in the windiest or sunniest parts of a country. At present, grids often become congested during peak renewable generation because there is little incentive to place demand near the source. Allowing prices to reflect local conditions would alleviate these bottlenecks and prevent wind turbines from being turned off during high winds. Ultimately, while there is no complete escape from natural gas in the near term, its ability to shock the global economy can be significantly reduced through strategic investments and smarter grid management.
